context_update.summary: This chunk presents the hot deformation behavior of an Fe-8% Al ferritic steel, covering material composition (Table 1), initial thermomechanical processing (three-pass rolling to ε=1, fully recrystallized equiaxed grains ~800 μm), and hot torsion testing parameters (900–1100 °C, 0.1–10 s⁻¹, argon quenching). EBSD characterization conditions are defined. Two dynamic recrystallization mechanisms are identified: (i) Continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) at 1000 °C and 0.1 s⁻¹, where subgrain boundaries progressively transform into high-angle boundaries with increasing strain, leading to crystallite aggregates and a strong D2 {1-12}<111> texture component (max at ε=15); (ii) Discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) at 1100 °C and high strain rates (5, 10 s⁻¹), producing small equiaxed substructure-free grains with random orientations and misorientation distribution close to MacKenzie random. A strain rate–temperature map (Figure 7) shows the competition between CDRX and DDRX. DDRX is proposed as beneficial for reducing roping defects. Key figures and tables: Figures 1–7, Table 1. Subsequent chunks should retain the processing history, CDRX/DDRX domains, texture outcomes, and the transition map.
material_systems: Fe-8% Al ferritic steel (non-ordered A2 lattice)
processing_route: Rolled in three passes, cumulative strain ε=1
microstructure: CDRX: subgrain → grain boundary transformation, crystallite aggregates
DDRX: small equiaxed recrystallized grains free of substructure, mixed with old elongated grains
Misorientation distributions (Fig. 2 for CDRX, Fig. 5 for DDRX)
properties: Texture: D2 component (max at ε=15) for CDRX; random texture for DDRX
mechanisms: CDRX: progressive transformation of subgrain boundaries into HABs at lower T and strain rate
DDRX: nucleation and growth of new grains by stored dislocation energy at higher T and strain rate

rolling_context_summary:
Summary: This chunk presents the hot deformation behavior of an Fe-8% Al ferritic steel, covering material composition (Table 1), initial thermomechanical processing (three-pass rolling to ε=1, fully recrystallized equiaxed grains ~800 μm), and hot torsion testing parameters (900–1100 °C, 0.1–10 s⁻¹, argon quenching). EBSD characterization conditions are defined. Two dynamic recrystallization mechanisms are identified: (i) Continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) at 1000 °C and 0.1 s⁻¹, where subgrain boundaries progressively transform into high-angle boundaries with increasing strain, leading to crystallite aggregates and a strong D2 {1-12}<111> texture component (max at ε=15); (ii) Discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) at 1100 °C and high strain rates (5, 10 s⁻¹), producing small equiaxed substructure-free grains with random orientations and misorientation distribution close to MacKenzie random. A strain rate–temperature map (Figure 7) shows the competition between CDRX and DDRX. DDRX is proposed as beneficial for reducing roping defects. Key figures and tables: Figures 1–7, Table 1. Subsequent chunks should retain the processing history, CDRX/DDRX domains, texture outcomes, and the transition map.
Material systems: Fe-8% Al ferritic steel (non-ordered A2 lattice)
Processing route: Rolled in three passes, cumulative strain ε=1; Hot torsion at 900–1100 °C, 0.1–10 s⁻¹; Argon quenching within 0.5 s at ~200 °C/s
Key variables: Strain (ε=1, 2, 5, 25); Strain rate (0.1, 5, 10 s⁻¹); Temperature (1000, 1100 °C)
Microstructure: Initial equiaxed grains ~800 µm; CDRX: subgrain → grain boundary transformation, crystallite aggregates; DDRX: small equiaxed recrystallized grains free of substructure, mixed with old elongated grains; Misorientation distributions (Fig. 2 for CDRX, Fig. 5 for DDRX)
Properties: Texture: D2 component (max at ε=15) for CDRX; random texture for DDRX; Density ~7 g/cm³, 10% weight reduction; Reduced roping tendency with DDRX
Mechanisms: CDRX: progressive transformation of subgrain boundaries into HABs at lower T and strain rate; DDRX: nucleation and growth of new grains by stored dislocation energy at higher T and strain rate; Competition between CDRX and DDRX
Figures or tables: Figure 1; Figure 2; Figure 3; Figure 4; Figure 5; Figure 6; Figure 7; Table 1
